target: Section 4.8 (Solar Angular Diameter) in generate-html.js, lines 1199-1207
replacement_html:
4.8 Solar Angular Diameter: The Simplest Test

This may be the simplest observational test anyone can perform. The dome model places the sun at an altitude of 5,733 km (labeled 'optical' by the model) or below 4,200 km ('physical,' below the firmament). At either altitude, the sun's distance to an observer changes dramatically through the day. A camera, a solar filter, and a ruler are sufficient to test whether the dome's geometry matches the sky. (Section 4.5.4 derives the same conclusion from the dome's internal equations; here we focus on the direct observational test.)

The geometry. On the dome disc, the sun orbits the pole at a radius of approximately 7,400 km (the Tropic of Cancer in dome coordinates). For an observer beneath the sub-solar point at noon, the sun is directly overhead at distance H = 5,733 km. As the sun moves along its orbit, the observer-to-sun distance increases: at a 60° hour angle (four hours from noon), the horizontal separation is 7,400 km and the line-of-sight distance grows to 9,360 km — a 63% increase. At a 90° hour angle (six hours from noon, mid-afternoon), horizontal separation reaches 10,465 km and line-of-sight distance climbs to 11,930 km — more than double the noon distance. Angular diameter scales inversely with distance (θ ≈ Dsun / d for small angles). A factor-of-two distance increase means the sun should appear half its noon size — a 52% reduction in angular diameter by mid-afternoon.

If the dome's 'physical' sun altitude is used instead (below 4,200 km, as stated on the model page), the predicted variation is even worse. A lower sun at the same orbital radius means the distance ratio between noon and any given hour angle is larger. At a physical altitude of 4,200 km and 90° hour angle, the line-of-sight distance reaches 11,280 km — a factor of 2.69, predicting a 63% size reduction. The dome cannot escape by invoking a lower physical altitude; it only deepens the falsification.

What we observe. Over a single day, the sun's apparent diameter varies by less than 0.01%. Over a year, it varies by ±1.67%, matching Earth's slightly elliptical orbit (eccentricity 0.0167). The annual variation is a distance effect — Earth is 3.3% closer to the sun at perihelion than aphelion — spread over six months. There is no daily variation mechanism on a globe: the sun is 150 million km away, and the observer's position on Earth's surface changes the distance by at most 6,400 km through the day, a 0.004% effect. Solar diameter constancy has been measured by micrometer since the 1830s (Royal Observatory Greenwich, 1836–1953) and confirmed by satellite (SDO/HMI, 2010–present). The dome model predicts a 52% daily variation; 190 years of measurement show less than 0.01%. The prediction fails by a factor of 5,000.

The dome's defense: aetheric refraction. The dome model's AI Context Rule 11 states: 'The 5,733 km Sun altitude is an OPTICAL illusion from crepuscular ray convergence. Do NOT claim it physically crashes into the firmament.' The implied defense is that aetheric refraction preserves the sun's apparent angular size despite the changing distance — the same refraction invoked to explain crepuscular ray convergence, stellar parallax suppression, and sunset itself. For this defense to work, the refraction must compensate a factor-of-two distance change to within 0.01% precision. This requires the refraction to magnify the sun's apparent diameter by exactly 1/cos(θ) as a function of zenith angle — at all observer latitudes, all times of day, all seasons. The dome model never specifies this functional form for aetheric refraction; it provides no formula, no physical mechanism, and no independent measurement of the effect. Invoking an undefined correction factor that must coincidentally replicate the exact angular scaling of a distant sun converts this from a testable prediction into an unfalsifiable assertion. For the full analysis of why aetheric refraction as an escape hatch undermines the dome's claim to be a scientific model, see Section 1.5 and Section 4.9.

The W021 smoking gun. The dome's own predictions page registers W021: 'Moon angular diameter variation: >2% moonrise vs transit (PENDING).' The dome explicitly predicts that its local moon (altitude 2,534 km) should produce observable angular diameter variation from geometry — the same geometric effect we describe above for the sun. But the dome registers no corresponding prediction for solar angular diameter variation, despite the sun being at a comparable or greater altitude. The dome's author applies the geometric argument selectively: predicting diameter variation where some variation genuinely exists (the moon varies by ~12% due to its elliptical orbit) while carefully omitting the same prediction for the sun, where near-perfect constancy would falsify the local geometry. This is not an oversight — it is evidence that the dome's author understands the problem and has chosen not to register the prediction where it would fail.

What you can verify. Photograph the sun through a solar filter at noon and again four hours later. Measure the disc diameter in pixels. On a globe, the two images will be identical within your measurement uncertainty. On a dome with a local sun, the afternoon image should show a sun 35–40% smaller. No telescope is needed; a telephoto lens and a safe solar filter are sufficient. This is a test that any person, anywhere, can perform in a single afternoon.
holes_addressed: MAJOR (ISS-418): Removed self-contradictory '50% AND 30%' numbers. Replaced with single rigorous calculation from dome parameters: 52% at 90° HA (optical altitude), 39% at 60° HA. All numbers derived from verified geometry (H=5733 km, r_tropic=7400 km).

MAJOR (ISS-419): Added full paragraph addressing dome's aetheric refraction defense. Cites AI Context Rule 11 explicitly. Shows refraction must compensate 2× distance change to 0.01% precision with exact 1/cos(θ) scaling — no formula provided, no mechanism specified, no independent measurement. Cross-references Sections 1.5 and 4.9.

MAJOR: Differentiated from Section 4.5.4. Opening paragraph now explicitly states '4.5.4 derives from dome internal equations; here we focus on the direct observational test.' Section 4.8 is structured as: geometry → observation → refraction defense → W021 evidence → DIY verification.
MODERATE: Added W021 asymmetry argument — dome predicts moon diameter variation but omits solar prediction. Shows selective application of dome's own geometry.
MODERATE: Replaced 'at sunset' with specific geometric conditions ('60° hour angle,' '90° hour angle,' 'four hours from noon'). No globe-dependent terminology.
MODERATE: Added paragraph on physical sun altitude (<4,200 km) making the problem worse — 63% reduction at 90° HA vs 52% at optical altitude.
MINOR: Separated daily vs annual variation explicitly. Daily: <0.01% (globe mechanism: 0.004%). Annual: ±1.67% from eccentricity. Dome predicts 52% daily — factor of 5,000 discrepancy.
new_evidence_added: Verified dome geometry: at 90° HA, observer-sun distance = 11,930 km (ratio 2.08, 52% size reduction)
Physical altitude calculation: at H=4,200 km and 90° HA, ratio = 2.69, 63% size reduction
Globe daily variation quantified: 6,400 km / 150,000,000 km = 0.004%
190 years of solar diameter measurement: Greenwich 1836–1953, SDO/HMI 2010–present
W021 prediction asymmetry: dome predicts >2% moon diameter variation but has no solar diameter prediction
AI Context Rule 11 cited directly as evidence of pre-built defense
Quantitative refraction burden: must match 1/cos(θ) to 0.01% at all positions/times/seasons
anticipated_objections: Dome: 'Aetheric refraction preserves angular constancy — the 5,733 km altitude is an optical illusion' → Addressed directly. Refraction must compensate a 2× distance change to 0.01% precision with 1/cos(θ) scaling. No formula specified, no independent measurement, no physical mechanism. This converts a testable prediction into an unfalsifiable assertion.

Dome: 'You're assuming straight-line optics in a refractive medium' → The dome also uses straight-line optics to derive the 5,733 km altitude from crepuscular ray convergence. The model cannot selectively invoke refraction where it helps and ignore it where it doesn't.
Dome: 'Sunset is a globe concept — our sun recedes via refraction' → We don't use 'sunset' anywhere. All comparisons use hour angles (60°, 90°) which are purely geometric and model-independent.

Dome: 'The moon also shows constant angular diameter through the night, and we handle that' → W021 explicitly predicts >2% moon diameter variation. The dome expects variation for the moon but avoids predicting it for the sun. If refraction preserves angular constancy for both, W021 should predict <2% moon variation, not >2%.

Dome: 'Section 4.8 and 4.5.4 make the same argument — you're padding' → They are explicitly differentiated: 4.5.4 derives from dome internal equations (self-contradiction), 4.8 presents the observational test anyone can perform. Different evidence, different framing, same devastating conclusion.
kernel_of_truth: The dome's intuition that a propagation medium could affect apparent angular sizes is physically sound. Real atmospheric refraction does change the sun's apparent shape — differential refraction flattens the solar disc vertically by ~0.5% near the horizon and displaces its apparent position by ~0.57° (a full solar diameter). The concept of a refractive medium affecting apparent sizes is established physics. However, real atmospheric refraction operates in the wrong direction for the dome (enlarges the apparent disc at the horizon, doesn't preserve it), affects only the lowest 5° of elevation, and produces distortion 400× smaller than what the dome needs. The dome's 'aetheric refraction' must do the opposite of real refraction, over the entire sky, to a precision of 0.01%, with no specified formula.

section_differentiation_from_4_5_4.section_4_5_4_focus: INTERNAL self-contradiction: the dome's own geometry (H(r), sun orbit, disc dimensions) produces predictions the dome's own WIN list doesn't address. Framed as 'the dome's equations refute the dome.'
section_differentiation_from_4_5_4.section_4_8_focus: OBSERVATIONAL falsification: here's the exact test, here's what the dome predicts, here's what 190 years of measurement show, here's why the refraction defense fails, here's the W021 smoking gun, here's how you can verify it yourself. Framed as 'anyone can test this in an afternoon.'
section_differentiation_from_4_5_4.overlap_minimized: Opening paragraph of 4.8 explicitly cross-references 4.5.4 for the internal-equations derivation. No dome equations appear in 4.8 — only observational geometry and measurement.

verification_notes.annual_variation: Eccentricity e = 0.0167. Diameter varies as 1/(1±e). Total range ≈ 2e = 3.34%, i.e., ±1.67%. ✓
verification_notes.w021_confirmed: raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 61: 'W021: Moon angular diameter variation: >2% moonrise vs transit (PENDING)'. No solar angular diameter prediction exists in the predictions list. ✓
verification_notes.rule_11_confirmed: raw-text/07-ai-context.txt line 24: '11. The 5,733 km Sun altitude is an OPTICAL illusion from crepuscular ray convergence. Do NOT claim it physically crashes into the firmament (roof is ~4,300 km).' ✓
verification_notes.real_refraction_direction: Real atmospheric refraction LIFTS the bottom edge of the solar disc more than the top, causing vertical compression (flattening). This makes the sun appear LARGER (wider/taller ratio changes, but overall disc area is preserved or slightly enlarged). The dome needs the opposite — apparent preservation despite receding. Real refraction works in the wrong direction. ✓
